title: Tall coil cylinder vase in air-dry clay
subtitle: A straight-sided vase built with coils over three sessions
excerpt: A tall coil cylinder is a more demanding version of the coil pot bowl: the walls are straight rather than curved, the height is greater, and keeping the walls truly vertical takes patience. Built over three short sessions as lower walls stiffen before upper coils are added. No kiln or wheel is needed. A glass jar inside holds fresh flowers.
sourceNotes: Coil cylinder technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: cylinder is harder than a wide bowl: the walls carry their own weight without the outward brace of a flared form. The technique is the same,

each coil, blend the interior, but the pauses between sessions matter. Adding too many coils before the lower section stiffens is what makes cylinder walls bulge. Build in three sessions over three days and the vase holds its form.

attrs.body: Air-dry clay softens when wet. Use a glass jar or small glass inside the vase to hold water for fresh-cut stems. The clay exterior remains intact as long as water is contained.
attrs.title: For fresh flowers

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 10 mm guide rails

content.text: Roll a slab 10 mm thick. Cut a circle 12 cm in diameter for the base. Compact the slab with the wooden rib from the centre outward to close any air pockets.
Step 2: Add the first four coils

Roll coils 15 mm in diameter. Score the perimeter of the base, apply slurry, and press the first coil around the edge. Blend the interior seam immediately. Add three more coils in the same way. After each new coil, check that the cylinder is vertical by sighting down from above, the rim should be a true circle, not an oval. Cover with plastic and leave overnight.
Step 3: Add the next four coils

, the lower walls now hold their shape under the weight of new coils. Add four more, each time pressing the coil slightly inside the previous one to keep the cylinder straight rather than flaring outward. Use the wooden rib pressed against the exterior to push any outward bulges back in. Cover and leave overnight again.

Add three or four final coils to reach the target height, around 25 to 28 cm for a tall stem vase. Trim the top coil level with the needle tool. Compress the rim with a wet finger to seal and round the edge. Dry bone-dry over three to four days. Sand with 120-grit sandpaper and seal with PVA inside and out.
Leave the exterior coil ridges unblended for a ribbed texture that shows the construction.
Flare the top two coils slightly outward to create a slight rim flare.

attrs.intro: Problems in building the tall cylinder.

items.fix: Wait until the lower section is leather-hard before continuing. Press the bulge back with the wooden rib while the clay is still workable.
items.cause: Coils added before the lower walls stiffened sufficiently.
items.symptom: Walls bulge outward in the middle section

items.fix: Check the circle from above after each coil. Correct while the coil is still fresh by pushing the wall inward at the wide points.
items.cause: Coils were positioned slightly off-centre when pressed on.
items.symptom: Cylinder is oval rather than round when viewed from above

attrs.heading: Common problems
